speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Kim Feinberg. I am interviewing Motel Pogir in Johannesburg, Gauteng, South Africa, on the 11th of November, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And spell it for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Motel Pogir, P-O-G-I-R, M-O-T-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motel, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lithuania in 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whereabout in Lithuania?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [INAUDIBLE], Krekenava, and a couple of months later we were deported [BACKGROUND NOISE] to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Krekenava for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krekenava is K-R-A-N-O-V-- Krekenava, V-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motel, can you tell me a little bit about your background, about your parents and what they did, as well as your school years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We came-- we, we came back from Russia. We came back in 1921. I was then seven years old, started to go to kindergarten. My parents, my mom was a housewife, and my father was in the transport business.[BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of those years in Russia, and whereabout in Russia were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia? Yeah. In Russia, we were in a place by the name called Kazan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kazan is K-A-Z-A-N. And I remember very little of then because I was a young little kid, but what I remember is, through Kazan, it goes through a very big river, which the river used to freeze in winter. The wintertime in, in Russia, the rivers used to freeze.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to existence, for working for an existence, my parents used to send me and a brother of mine, [INAUDIBLE], and we used to chop out a hole in the ice, and the people who used to bring food to the big cities,[BACKGROUND NOISE] the farmers, they used to stop at our place and give it a drink to their-- to their horses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for that, they used to give us a couple eggs, a piece of butter, a piece of cheese. And we made from that just a living, a little bit. Then, when we came back, we started to rebuild our house-- which it was burned down-- and reopen the bakery. I obviously was in, in the years, from school, kindergarten, and then I started to [INAUDIBLE], which is a Hebrew school, Yiddish and Hebrew. And in my later years, when I finished school, I joined the Hashomer Hatzair, organization, a Zionist organization, where we were prepared ourselves to go to Israel. In 1932, year '33, I read in the Lithuanian paper that they're opening courses for electricians. So I walked-- our shtetl was, from Ponevezh, 30 kilometers. So I walked to Ponevezh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ponevezh was P-O-N-I-Z-E-- Ponevezh, yeah. And I went to-- it was a technical college, and I finished the technical college. and I was going-- I was then joined the Halutz [INAUDIBLE], which I was two years a Halutz to go to Israel. And something happened to my certificate when it came about. It was sold over my, my head, that certificate, for some people who had more money than us, and somebody else immigrated instead of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motel, how many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were five brothers and three sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whereabout did you fit in? What age group were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was then about 17, 18 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers and sisters, can you name them? Yeah, I was the youngest. A sister I had, Faga, which she was 19. Then a brother, Simon. He was 21. Every second year we-- shall I go through all of them? And this is Simon. And then we had Chaim, a brother, and a sister again, Tybie, and another sister, Pesia. And then one brother Avraham, and then Shlomo, he was the oldest one. And did you come from a religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We-- in this small shtetlach, there wasn't any other life. Only it was a religious life, with shul three times a day, shacharit, minchah, maariv, and Hebrew was no problem to us. I even speak now, Hebrew. I understand well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your rabbi's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shloma-- Shloma [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So there wasn't much anti-Semitism that you came across, was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: While we were young people, we didn't feel a bad anti-Semitism, but occasionally we went-- we went in with a fight with some Lithuanian people. Like, example, I was a member of the fire brigade in, in the Krekenova. In wintertime, we used to make ice skate rinks, chop out little holes in the ice, and plant little trees around, and that was the ice rink. We used to charge 20 cents for, for ice skating. And one day, from the Lithuanian school, there were a number of students, and they started to, to fight with me and my brother, Simon. We were quite tough guys, the two of us, and we took off the ice skates, and we gave them a good hiding with the ice skates. We were called in by the police to apologize to them because some of them got hurt badly. But when we appeared before the headmaster of the school, and he ask us what brought us to the fight, and when I said to them that they called us [NON-ENGLISH]. That means [INAUDIBLE], like [NON-ENGLISH]. [INAUDIBLE], and then we start fighting. They started to push us around. Obviously, we didn't look just to-- with our heads down. We fight back. And if this is the case, he says, you can go back. That's our student's fault and the students were actually prosecuted, not us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the transport business. He had a horse and cart, and he used to transport grain from Krekenova to Ponevezh. It was the capital there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 30 kilometers, dirt road. The people, used to-- they used to walk, not sit on the wagon, but they used to walk past-- past the road and walk right through the night to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your home, Motel, tell me a little bit about your home, what it looked like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our home, we had quite a nice house. Our house was the only house in the shtetl with an iron roof. The other houses were thatched roofs. And we then opened a bakery, and my sisters and brother-- one brother, especially-- and myself, we used to work in the bakery and help in the bakery as much as possible. But most of it was my older brother, Simon, which he is not alive anymore, and the two sisters, Faga and Tybie. These two, They were the main bakers from the family. And we had a big garden. My mother used to work in the garden most of the days, and we grew quite a lot of cucumbers and fruit, and this and that, and we used to sell them and make a living off, out of that. That was the living. We had a cow, and milk and cheese and everything was there. And we lived quite-- until--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's names, Motel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chava. Chava.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also Chava but Pogir was afterward, Chava Pogir.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what was her name before Pogir? Because she was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Friedman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Friedman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Friedman, sorry. Friedman. And in 1935, I went into Lithuanian army. I was in Lithuanian army for a year and a half in the cavalry. I was the only Jewish boy amongst 180 Christian guys. And nobody knew that I'm Jewish, because I looked blond. My language was good. I used to speak Lithuanian, Polish, Russian, German. We had some, all these people in the army. And after I finished the army, I came back home, but I didn't stay long. And I went to Kovna for a job. I got myself a job in Kovna. I walked to Kovna, 105 kilometers, me and a friend of mine by the name [INAUDIBLE]. We walked it real fast. It took us two days, 105 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: We slept on the side of the road, and when we came to Kovna, we start walking around to look for a job. We stayed in a [INAUDIBLE], because we were [? halutzing ?] from the [INAUDIBLE], so they accommodate us. And I got a job when I-- the boss asked me, how much do you want a month? I said to him, whatever you-- whatever you'll offer me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started off working. That was in 1937. And I worked myself up very nicely, because I was-- first of all, I was known by the electrical everything. We had an electrical business. And I got well paid, and then in six months time, I earned, already, 140 litas. From 80, I started off for 80 litas, and 80 litas was like one pound ten, South African money, those days. But I worked myself up very nicely, and in a short while I worked, I got 240, 300, 350 and so on, until the 22nd of June, 1940. Russia walked in into Lithuania. And the [NON-ENGLISH] commissar of the Russian appointed me to be the commissar of the shop. We were 16 people in that business. And we had seven storerooms of goods in, in the business, spread all over places. And after a couple of months later, I received a letter from the Russian authorities to get rid of my [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how old were  you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was then 27.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married at this stage, or not yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't married then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, being the commissar of the shop, and I went up with a letter to show to my owners of the business, and they started to cry. They went up to the hanger of the wall, and took their jackets, and walked out crying. We then took stock of the businesses, and one big storeroom, I have not declared. I took a chance not to declare one storeroom of goods, and my [INAUDIBLE] boss used to come to the certain times we used to arrange, from me, and I used to give him goods from there, to help him out to make a living. And he used to sell to the goods to us under a false name, and we used to pay him back. Because goods, goods started to be very scarce for when Russia moved in, very, very scarce. Everything was sold out to the shops in no time. And from Russia, we didn't get any goods at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living in our own home at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was then living with a friend of mine, which we were friends from the army. We're living together. His name was Alter Eisen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was working for a manufacturing business. And we were quite good friends, and we lived together in a, in a quite in a nice room, until the war broke out in 1941, the 22nd of June. That morning, me and my friend, we went fishing. I had a motorbike. So we went fishing together, and we saw a lot of planes flying around. The fishing was about 25 kilometers away from Kovna. And we didn't recognize the planes. That's not Russian planes, I said to my mate. Look at all these Russians. They're mad today. Look at the planes what that's running around. And they were German planes. Because, 3 o'clock in the morning, Germany attacked Lithuania, Estonia, Latvia, these three little countries. And the fishing was bad. They didn't bite, the fish, so I said to my mate, let's go home. The planes are flying around, I suppose they frightened the fish away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went-- we didn't go straight from there home, but we went down the bridge near Kovna, also the river by the name [INAUDIBLE], and we went and we stopped. We may catch fish there. But it was a bad day for fish catching, and the planes were still intensive, more flying around, and we picked up and we come back. When we came back to my flat, my wife, Ida, was standing there and crying-- I married in 1940-- was standing outside the flat and crying with the words, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], where did you got lost? And, well, we explained to her that fishing was bad. We try this way, that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, a day later, my motorbike was confiscated. The fighting intensified. The Germans started to bomb Kovna, so we started to run towards Russia. A lot of people, also, started to run towards Russia. And my friend was visiting [INAUDIBLE] Kovna, that [INAUDIBLE]. I mentioned him before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came to me crying that he wants to go home, no transport. I said, come, I'll give you a bicycle from the shop. I still had the keys from the shop. I'll give you a bicycle from the shop, and go. But unfortunately, he did not arrive. They killed him and took him away, and took away the bicycle from him. Motel, I'm going to take you back a little bit. Tell me about your wife Ida, how you met her, when you got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. With Ida, we were in [INAUDIBLE]. We grew up together in [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] as kids, you know. And then the shtetl was small, and we belonged to the same organization, [INAUDIBLE] Hatzair.[BACKGROUND NOISE] And we became friendly, and more, and more, and more, and then I went away to Kovna for a job. She was still remained in Krekenava. She had her own little shop there, left by her sisters who immigrated to Israel, and she run that little shop. And then when we got married, she came to Kovna, and we live together, obviously, in Kovna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a traditional Jewish marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we went to the rabbi-- to a rabbi-- and he gave us the [NON-ENGLISH], what they calls? And we were quite happily married and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did her family do, Motel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her family, they were grain buyers, grain-- buying and selling grain. Quite a good business, they had. And our bakery, our family used to buy flour from them. So also, we used to visit the family every day. We were, we were quite good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters did she have?

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Did she had? She, she was from seven sisters and one brother. And one brother, yeah. She had two sisters in Israel, which they survived. The one is still alive, and one died a couple years ago. And the rest of it went with the transport to Ponevezh, which they were all slaughtered. They were slaughtered near the army barracks, which, Ponevezh, I knew very well. And I went to visit them after. I went after the war, in 1944, when the war ended. I went to Ponevezh, and I went to quite a number of small places. Me and a friend of mine, we were traveling around looking for Jewish-- Jewish children who survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motel, let's go back a little bit. Tell me, did you know about the Nazis before you were invaded?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You hadn't heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew where Germany was, a neighbor of Lithuania. A neighbor of near. We didn't know about Nazis, but we used to hear Hitler, Hitler talking on the radio. He was a terrific speaker that used to absolutely-- it was just shiver the walls when he used to talk. [SPEAKING GERMAN], he used to shout, [SPEAKING GERMAN] and this and that. And that was, obviously, started to be at bedtime for Tuesday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever think that they would invade your country?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were invaded by the Russians in 1940, the Russian army, the propaganda of the Russians was terrific. We thought that Russia will conquer the world. So the army, we haven't seen any better armies, so we thought that that's a terrific army. And the propaganda were terrific. So the Russians used to say, if any war, we'll win the war with trying capture the Germans. They'll win the war. But fortunately, when the war started, Hitler had a terrific army. And in a matter of three days, they invaded Lithuania, and not one of the Russian army soldiers and officers escaped from, from Lithuania. Not one of them. Most of them got slaughtered.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first impression?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first impression was that that's the end of our, our Jewish nation. Because, when we, we managed to run, that after two or three days running, on the way, the Germans used to come down with Messerschmitt, it used to call-- the plane-- over the heads of the people who were running and machine gun them. We were-- fortunately, we didn't-- we were in the same groups, but we used to low on the ground, fall to the ground, and they, they missed us. But a day later, they overrunned us. We were 35 kilometers away from Kovna. And then, obviously, the trouble started. Big trouble started. A month later, we were locked up in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened during that month period, Motel?

speaker: interviewee
text: That period of that month? Jewish were prosecuted straight away. It was difficult for us to get out to any shops to get food. And they started to arrest, mostly, the men. We were hiding out like I when-- when they used to come and I used to hide in the [INAUDIBLE]. And they used to come in, the Germans, and look for men. And my wife Ida was then pregnant, and they had sympathy to hear that. When she used to go out, and she used to go out shopping, she used to come with some bread, or this or that. That was hard. And then, we were living in the ghetto. The ghetto was locked up. We were living-- we were 36,000 people. And the daily problem started in the, in the ghetto. By going to ghetto, they took away all our clothing. Whatever we carry of clothing, we had to cross a bridge. To go to Kovna, to the Slobodka ghetto, we had to cross a bridge. And on that bridge used to be police, mostly Lithuanian, and they used to take away from us the suitcases, everything. We went into the ghetto, we were-- whatever we had on ourselves. Some managed to get through a little bit. Like, I managed a little bit to get through. But most of it were taken away. [SIGHS] And things went on very bad. Ten days later, when we were locked up, we got a job. They came to the [NON-ENGLISH], which it was the Work Bureau. And the German demanded 10, 10 people, electrician, a plumber, and some eight ordinary, without any. And I was an electrician, so I had the job. And a plumber was also, by the name [INAUDIBLE], his name was. And the job was in a brothel, believe it or not. Unbelievable, but it was quite reasonable there. There were 35 girls, Polish, Lithuanian. And we used to clean the floors and wash the floors, and all these sort of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: My job was to connect a radio, repair this, a plug, or this or that electrical. And the plumber, also, something there. And that was when we-- we didn't overwork there. And the girls used to have quite a lot of money. Shall I say it in details, more in details? Tell me, were the girls Jewish, Motel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were no Jewish girls. There were only Lithuanian and Polish. They wouldn't accept Jewish girls, because, they said, [NON-ENGLISH]. So they wouldn't accept Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the-- it was for only the soldiers. It was the-- the brothel was only for ordinary soldiers. They used to come in at the door. They used to be examined and paid three marks, two marks goes to the girl, and one mark to the doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motel, tell me, why were there no Jewish girls allowed in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Germans did not allow any Jewish mixed blood.
